The SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant isn't connected to the correct SAP BTP Global Account, or it's linked to the wrong customer ID.
How to Fix it:
a. Make sure your IAS tenant is linked to the correct Global Account.
Check the SAP Help:View Assigned Tenants and Admins.

b. If you have multiple IAS tenants , use the IAS tenant that matches the Global Account's customer ID.
Important:Data syncing between systems usually takes up to one week.Wait for this to complete before running the booster again so that the SAP IAS system appears in all the accounts.

You can manually trigger the sync via the “Refresh” button.
SAP Note 3620409 - Could not find SAP Cloud Identity system with host: Joule booster error

Enabling Joule leads to the following error: 
"Prerequisites for the formation type "Integration with Joule" are not fulfilled.
No SAP Cloud identity service system is showing up under“System landscape” --->System

 

A new SAP BTP Global Account will take some time to synchronize all existing customer systems, but you can manually trigger the sync via the "Refresh" button.
This should ensure that your SAP Cloud Identity Services system shows up. Afterwards you can proceed with the Joule configuration.

 

The SAP Build Work Zone system does not appear in the dropdown list during the setup process (Joule Booster - Step 5 Set Up Integrations).

 

 

The SAP Build Work Zone service was not subscribed to under the relevant subaccount.
An instance of this service is not enough for the integration; therefore, the system will not appear under the options.

Add the SAP Build Work Zone subscription in the Joule subaccount. Refer to
SAP Note 3710864 - SAP Build Work Zone system not visible in dropdown during setup

Unable to find the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition option in Step 3 - "Select Integration" stage dropdown during activation by means of the Joule Booster.

In global account Systems, the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system is already auto-discovered.

Ensure that it is visible in Joule Booster Step 04  "Select Capabilities", select "S/4HANA Private Cloud" and proceed to complete the Joule booster.

Refer to SAP Note 3710921 - S/4HANA Private Cloud option is not listed in Step 3 - "Select Integration" in Joule Booster for detailed information.

Error message: "The subscription of subaccount <xxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxx> to application das-application and plan foundation, failed with state: SUBSCRIBE_FAILED."

Observe the error messages indicating SaaS Subscription failed and instructions to open a support case or unsubscribe and retry.

 

Refer to SAP Note 3689452 - Entity Saas Subscription failed after running Joule Booster for detailed instructions.

If the issue persists, raise a support incident and choose component CA-JOULE-PRV-ONB for further investigation.

 

 

 3. UX/ Icon appearance issue

Joule refused to frame 'https://xxx.xxx.cloud.sap/' because an ancestor violates the following Content Security Policy directive: "frame-ancestors”.                             

 

 

 

 

Enable 3rd party cookies of your browser.Add the trusted domain in SAP IAS and SAP BTP
a. Add trusted domain in SAP IAS in the SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant.
Go to Applications & Resources -->Tenant Settings --> Trusted Domains--> Click the + (Add) button
Type the URL of the trusted host into the field.

b. Add the trusted domain in SAP BTP.
In SAP BTP cockpit, navigate to your specific subaccount ---> Security ---> Settings.
Click the + (Add) button.
Enter the required hostname, for example: https://<your-s4-or-sfsf-url>.com

The Joule icon at the top app bar in the Fiori Launchpad is not properly displayed.

The issue is caused using an unsupported SAPUI5 version in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system. 

 

Upgrade the SAPUI5 Component version of SAP S/4HANA to be equal to or higher than the minimum supported version.

SAP Notes:
2346367 - How to upgrade the SAPUI5 library in ABAP - SAP for Me
3641945 - Joule icon displayed wrongly in Fiori launchpad of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 
 

After integrating Joule with SAP Solutions, the user is unable to launch Joule by clicking the diamond icon,  it fails to load or respond.

 

 

Allow third-party cookies.

SAP Note 3643889 - User unable to launch the Joule Icon after integrating with SAP Solutions

The Joule icon is not appearing for a specific user despite that the correct role-based permissions have been assigned in SAP SuccessFactors.

The issue is most likely caused by security policies of the network provider, which may be blocking certain URLs that are required for the icon to appear.
The affected user does not have the General User Permission --->Access to Joule

Refer to SAP Note 3685621 - Joule button not appearing on SuccessFactors

After clicking on the Joule icon in SAP SuccessFactors, it tries to load for a few minutes and subsequently the user gets a blank or greyed-out screen.

Refer to SAP Note 3544482 - Getting Error / White /Blank / IAS login Screen When Trying to Launch Joule in SuccessFactors

4. SAP IPS/IAS issues

The SAP IAS source system read job is failing.

 

Keep the source system name the same as in the content channel that had been created in the standard SAP Build Work Zone.

Joule was successfully integrated with the SAP product. However, clicking the Joule icon results in a blank white or SAP IAS login screen. Single sign-on is not working.

 

Check the SAP IAS domain. In the SAP BTP Joule subaccount, go to Trust Configuration and select the Custom Identity Provider for Applications.

Change the field "Domain" to the one used in the SAP product.

Refer to SAP Note 3635949 - Blank white / IAS login screen appears when clicking on Joule icon from the solutions where Joule was integrated into

5. Restrictions /Constraints
SAP Joule operates within specific constraints.
SAP notes:
3698807 - How to Remove or Hide the Joule Icon in SAP S/4HANA Cloud
3711505 - Restrictions: Multiple different Work Zone editions integration with Joule
Before starting the Joule project, please refer to the document below. All constraints applicable to Joule are listed here.
SAP Help:Joule constraints

6. Monitoring and troubleshooting
You may experience issues that would have to be reported in SAP incidents. In such cases, please collect information, which will help the support team to investigate and resolve the problem quickly.
SAP Note 3666014 - Information required for Jouletroubleshooting
SAP Help:Monitoring and Troubleshooting

7. How to report issues
You can report an incident or error through SAP for Me. Ensure that you provide a detailed description of the issue including the steps to reproduce it. Attach any relevant screenshots, logs, or error messages to help with the investigation. Please use the appropriate components listed below when creating your incident.

General support component for core Joule services issue and functionality

CA_JOULE

Joule integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

CA-FLP-EXT-PCE-JOULE

SaaS Subscription error after running Joule Booster

CA-JOULE-PRV-ONB

8. Frequently asked questions

  1. Are there options available to customize the theme of Joule so it aligns with our branding guidelines and specific colour palette?

The theme tab is only available when Joule is used in a standalone (single page) mode. For now, Joule automatically adapts to the theme of the embedding application.  For example, if the user will changes the theme in the SAP Work Zone settings, Joule will automatically adopt it. The user cannot choose a different theme for the Joule client.
SAP Note 3646989 - Is it possible to adjust theming for Joule?

  1. Is it possible to remove the Joule icon for selected users?

When Joule is enabled through the Joule booster, it is activated for all users in both SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud systems. At present, there is no supported option to restrict or disable Joule for specific users or user groups.
SAP Note 3698807 - How to Remove or Hide the Joule Icon in SAP S/4HANA Cloud

Summary
In this blog, we reviewed common configuration and activation issues related to Joule including challenges with SAP Business Technology Platform provisioning, Joule booster activation, UX visibility, and integration with SAP Cloud Identity Services (IPS/IAS), along with key restrictions, monitoring tips, and reference resources.

In addition, we discussed current system restrictions and limitations, monitoring options, log analysis techniques, and practical troubleshooting steps to help identify and resolve issues efficiently.

By following the guidance and best practices outlined above, administrators can streamline Joule activation, minimize configuration errors, and ensure a smoother user experience. If problems persist, contact SAP Support with detailed information such as error messages, screenshots, tenant details, and relevant logs to accelerate root cause analysis and resolution.

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and RIG team

entry.title: From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This March
entry.content:
In the age of AI, the skills we build today will define how we work tomorrow. That’s why we’ve launched a monthly blog series that spotlights the latest AI learning assets and upcoming learning opportunities.

Building on my first blog, where I introduced you to our AI live sessions Beyond Hype (which is all about getting productive with SAP Business AI), I wanted to address some of your most asked questions. But before I do that, I’m excited to share that the sessions are now ungated and no longer require a SAP Learning Hub subscription!

  1. What methodology should customers follow to modernize with RISE with SAP, and what environment prerequisites must customers meet to enable the AI toolchain?

    The recommended approach for modernization is the RISE with SAP methodology—a single, standardized framework that guides customers end-to-end across their transformation journey. Built on SAP Activate and enriched with proven best practices, it provides a structured path from discovery to run, while embedding clean core principles and quality gates to ensure consistency, governance, and partner alignment throughout. 

    This methodology is complemented by an integrated toolchain enabled by SAP Business AI. With the RISE with SAP package, key tools such as SAP Signavio, SAP LeanIX, and SAP Cloud ALM are already included, so that customers can start without additional licenses for the standard AI-powered setup. However, the bundled offering covers foundational capabilities. Advanced or specialized features in Signavio and LeanIX may require additional licenses.

    Beyond the commercial bundling, SAP also supports customers operationally through onboarding and the Transformation Preparation Service (TPS), helping set up and configure the toolchain within the customer’s landscape from day one.



  2. How are changes in solutions like SAP Signavio, SAP LeanIX, and SAP Cloud ALM automatically synchronized across the toolchain?

    SAP Signavio is the leading system for process modelling; it’s where processes are designed, planned, and maintained. These models are then pushed to SAP Cloud ALM, where they are used to run and manage projects. If changes to a process are required, they are made in SAP Signavio first, and the updated models are then re-synchronized with SAP Cloud ALM. This ensures a clear “single source of truth” in Signavio, with Cloud ALM always working on the latest version.

  3. What do I need to get started with Joule agents — from technical prerequisites to activation steps?

    Getting started with Joule agents is straightforward once you understand the discovery and provisioning process. Here's a high-level walkthrough:

    Step 1: Discover Available Agents
    Start by exploring the AI Catalog in SAP Discovery Center. As explained in the first question, there you'll find the complete list of available agents, along with details about their capabilities, availability status (GA, Early Adopter Care, or Beta), and pricing.

    Step 2: Evaluate and Trial
    If you find an agent that fits your needs, check whether it's available for trial. The Discovery Center provides all the necessary information, including technical prerequisites such as BTP subaccount requirements, entitlements, connectivity settings, and configuration details.

    Step 3: Provision the Agent
    Once your organization has purchased the required entitlements, you can provision the agent directly through SAP for Me. We're also introducing enhanced capabilities that will guide you through the provisioning process, including the selection of prerequisite landscapes. BTP will handle the necessary connections in the background, as these are already configured within your BTP landscape- making activation smooth and seamless.

    What if I am still on-premise? Joule agents are cloud-native and run on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). On-premise customers can access these agents by connecting their systems to BTP via integration solutions such as SAP Integration Suite or BTP connectivity services. This allows them to leverage the power of AI agents while keeping their core business systems on-premise.

    Read on to get answers to your top questions from the session in this blog.

In this three-part tutorial series, you can rewatch how Joule Agents embedded in core business processes such as CX, HR, and Finance help organizations move from insight to action. You’ll learn where agents can proactively support decisions, execute process steps, and reduce manual effort.

Human Resource Agent: Shift into a manager's perspective. In this demo, we’ll focus on how managers not only support their teams day to day but also track and evaluate performance over time. A key part of this is the regular review cycles, whether quarterly, mid-year, or annual, where managers assess progress, provide feedback, and guide their teams toward better outcomes. You’ll see how this process can be streamlined and enhanced with intelligent support.

 

Customer Experience Agent: Step into the role of a sales representative or sales manager within the Sales Cloud application. You’ll see how users can easily access their assigned leads and opportunities, and more importantly, how they can take the next best actions supported by intelligent sales and service agents. The focus is on how AI-driven insights help prioritize work, guide decision-making, and ultimately drive more efficient and effective sales execution.

 

Finance Agent: Let’s put ourselves in the shoes of an accountant. In this demo, we’ll focus on one of the more time-consuming aspects of finance, managing accruals during month-end, quarter-end, or year-end cycles. While finance should enable strategic insights, much of the effort often goes into repetitive administrative tasks like journal entries and estimations. You’ll see how an accounting accrual agent can take on this workload, helping automate entries, improve accuracy, and free up time for more value-driven activities.

 

Want to dive deeper into the content? Rewatch the session and access the session material:

  • Accelerate your transformation to the Cloud with SAP Business AI (recording) 
  • Efficient Document Inbound Processing in SAP Cloud ERP with SAP Document AI (recording)

Hi Joule, where should I get started with the latest learning resources? *

Apart from live enablement sessions, explore our latest on-demand learning resources:

  • Introducing Joule Learning Journey: Understand Joule and the business value it provides, its core components, role‑based context‑aware workflows, and adoption essentials.

* Powered by the machine learning technology of SAP AI Business Services, personalized recommendations are generated for employees, in one place on the Learning home page, based on employee data.

As we look ahead, we’re excited to share with you a new lineup of learning opportunities designed to help you expand your knowledge and make AI an advantage in your role:

  • April 8, 15:00 CET: Agent Assisted Coding with AI Core. Explore how to leverage SAP AI Core's Generative AI Hub to enable agent-assisted coding workflows in your enterprise development environment.
  • April 9, 10 am CET: Build AI securely -Avoid Pitfalls in the Development and Operations Lifecycle. Learn more about common security pitfalls across development and operations, from secret leaks in version control to missing checks and misconfigured infrastructure that expose credentials.
  • April 14, 3 pm CET: Activating and Monitoring SAP Business AI in SAP for Me. Learn how to activate SAP Business AI capabilities and monitor AI consumption using SAP for Me.

Stay tuned for more, and happy learning! 🚀

Further Resources:

  • From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This October
  • From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This November
  • From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This December
  • From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This January
  • From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This February

Joule Agentic AI business process transformation Security

entry.title: Governing the Emerging Collective Intelligence
entry.content:
Artificial intelligence is often described as a technological breakthrough. But what if it is something more fundamental? Today’s AI systems are trained on vast amounts of human-generated data, documents, transactions, conversations, and decisions. In doing so, they do not simply process information. They reorganise fragments of our shared intellectual and cultural history.

When AI systems retrieve and interpret this data, they surface not only knowledge, but also ambiguity, bias, contradiction, and unresolved human tension. What emerges is not purely artificial intelligence-it is a reflection of humanity itself. This raises a profound question: Are we building systems that replicate human reasoning, or systems that externalise our collective mind?

From Collective Unconscious to Data Lakes

Carl Jung introduced the idea of the collective unconscious—a deeper layer of shared psychological structures that shape how humans perceive, think, and act. Modern enterprises are, in many ways, building a technological equivalent. Data lakes, enterprise platforms, and AI systems aggregate vast amounts of collective experience—sales interactions, customer behaviour, operational decisions—into shared repositories. From these, patterns emerge. Insights form. Decisions are guided.

But there is a critical difference. The collective unconscious was invisible. Enterprise intelligence systems are not. They are queryable, operational, and increasingly autonomous. This shift transforms something deeply human into something actionable—and scalable.

The Mirror and the Shadow

If AI systems reflect our collective knowledge, they also reflect our limitations. They can amplify:

  • Biases embedded in historical data
  • Misaligned incentives within organisations
  • Incomplete or conflicting interpretations of reality

In this sense, AI is not just a tool. It is a mirror—one that shows both clarity and distortion. Without proper governance, this mirror does not simply reflect reality. It can reshape it.

From Intelligence to Responsibility

This is where the conversation must evolve. AI governance is often framed as a technical or regulatory concern—focused on compliance, accuracy, and control. These are necessary, but not sufficient. If AI represents an externalised form of collective intelligence, then governance becomes something more: AI governance is the stewardship of how human knowledge, decisions, and meaning are captured, interpreted, and amplified by machines.

This introduces new responsibilities for organisations:

  • Not only to manage data, but to understand what it represents
  • Not only to deploy AI, but to question what it reinforces
  • Not only to optimise outcomes, but to ensure alignment with human intent

The Enterprise Imperative

For enterprises, this shift is already happening. AI copilots, assistants, and agents are becoming embedded in daily workflows—supporting sellers, guiding decisions, and interpreting complex systems like compensation, forecasting, and customer engagement. These systems do more than automate tasks. They shape how people understand reality within the organisation. And that makes governance not optional—but foundational.

From SaaS to Governing Intelligence: The Role of Joule

Enterprise software is undergoing a fundamental transformation. For decades, SaaS systems were designed to store, process, and expose data. Their primary role was to provide structure, consistency, and transactional reliability across business processes. With the rise of AI, this role is expanding. Systems are no longer just systems of record or systems of engagement. They are becoming systems of interpretation—capable of explaining outcomes, guiding decisions, and interacting with users in natural language. This is where SAP Joule represents a significant shift. Joule is not simply an AI assistant embedded into enterprise applications. It acts as an interface to enterprise intelligence, connecting data, processes, and business context into a unified layer of understanding. But more importantly, Joule introduces the foundation for something new: A governed layer of enterprise intelligence.

As AI agents begin to interpret sensitive domains such as compensation, performance, customer interactions, and financial outcomes, the need for trusted interpretation becomes critical. This moves SAP’s role beyond delivering SaaS applications. It positions SAP as a provider of:

  • Context-aware intelligence, grounded in enterprise data
  • Traceable reasoning, connecting outputs back to underlying transactions
  • Governed AI interactions, aligned with business rules, policies, and intent

In this model, Joule is not only assisting users—it is operating within a controlled and explainable framework of enterprise knowledge. This is the transition:

  • From delivering applications
  • To enabling decisions
  • To govern how intelligence itself is produced and consumed within the enterprise

Why This Matters-As organisations adopt AI at scale, the risk is not only incorrect answers, but ungoverned meaning.

Without a governing layer, AI may generate plausible but ungrounded interpretations. Decisions may drift from policy and intent. Trust in enterprise systems may erode. With Joule and SAP’s AI foundation, there is an opportunity to ensure that:

  • Intelligence is grounded in enterprise truth
  • Outputs are explainable and auditable
  • AI becomes a trusted extension of the organisation, not an uncontrolled external layer

A Strategic Shift-This marks a broader transformation: From Software as a Service → to Governed Intelligence as a Capability

In this future, the value of enterprise platforms will not be defined only by the data they store—but by how responsibly they interpret and operationalise that data through AI.

A New Discipline

We are entering a new phase of AI adoption—one where the question is no longer what AI can do, but what it should represent. This requires a new discipline that connects:

  • Technology and psychology
  • Data and meaning
  • Automation and accountability

Because in the end, the systems we build will not only reflect our intelligence—They will reflect our values.

As enterprises continue to build and deploy AI, one question should remain at the centre: If AI becomes the voice of our collective intelligence—who ensures it speaks with clarity, responsibility, and truth?

entry.title: The Joule Effect: Event Recap ⚡️
entry.content:
Last week at the SAP Casablanca office, we brought together SAP professionals and enthusiasts for our event The Joule Effect, a focused session on the future of Business AI.

With the support of the SAP Community team, and contributions from @Salah2  and @belahmew, we explored what SAP Joule really means for our ecosystem.

Key Takeaways

  • AI is changing the game: Faster, simpler SAP interactions
  • Clean Core is essential: The foundation for scalable and secure AI adoption
  • Consultants remain key: Technology evolves, but expertise drives real transformation

Beyond the Content

What stood out most was the exchange between experienced professionals and emerging SAP talents. This is exactly what we want to build in Casablanca: a space to learn, connect, and grow together.

What’s Next

This is just the start. Our goal is to establish a consistent SAP knowledge hub in Morocco.

If you missed it:

  • Follow the Casablanca tag on SAP Community
  • Connect with us on LinkedIn
  • Share what topic we should cover next

Thank you to everyone who made this first step a success. More is coming.

entry.title: How SAP's AI copilot is moving from helper to agentic layer that reasons and acts inside HR workflow
entry.content:
Your organization likely spent the last two years deploying conversational AI as a convenience layer: a chat window that answers leave balance questions and helps employees find the right form. Useful. Incremental. Already obsolete.

The shift underway is not from chatbot to better chatbot. It is from tool to colleague. SAP's Joule is moving from a system that responds to one that reasons, plans, and acts, often before anyone asks it to. The implications for HR operating models are far more disruptive than most organizations have internalized.

Deloitte's State of AI in the Enterprise 2026 report found that nearly three-quarters of companies plan to deploy agentic AI within two years. Yet only 21% report having a mature governance model for autonomous agents.[1] That gap between ambition and readiness is where the real risk sits.

This piece examines what agentic AI actually means for HR, how Joule's architecture is evolving to deliver it, and what organizations need to get right before agents start making decisions alongside their people.

The shift from copilot to co-worker

The terminology matters, because the technology has outgrown the language most organizations use to describe it.

A chatbot retrieves information. You ask a question; it returns an answer. The interaction is bounded, reactive, and stateless.

A copilot augments human action. It drafts, summarizes, recommends. It operates within a single workflow at a user's request. Most of what organizations call "AI in HR" today lives here.

An agent does something categorically different. It reasons over a goal, decomposes it into steps, gathers information from multiple systems, makes decisions within defined guardrails, and takes action, often across process boundaries. Critically, it retains context, learns from outcomes, and can initiate work without being prompted.

SAP announced Joule in September 2023 as a generative AI copilot.[2] In the roughly two and a half years since, it has evolved into an agentic platform. The distinction is not academic. When Joule shifts from answering "What is my leave balance?" to proactively telling a manager "Three of your five critical successors have development plans that are stalled. Here's what I recommend," that is not a better chatbot. That is a new kind of organizational actor.

What makes agentic AI different

Deloitte's Tech Trends 2026 report frames the distinction sharply: organizations must begin treating AI agents as a "silicon-based workforce" that complements the human workforce, with dedicated management frameworks for onboarding, performance tracking, and cost management.[3] The metaphor is intentional. Agents are not features to be toggled on. They are workers that happen to run on silicon, and they require governance, oversight, and integration into team structures just as any new hire would.

Most organizations remain early in this transition. While 30% are exploring agentic options and 38% are piloting solutions, only 14% have solutions ready for deployment, and a mere 11% are actively using these systems in production.[4] Legacy system integration, data architecture constraints, and the absence of enterprise-grade governance remain the primary barriers.

For HR, the distinction between copilot and agent carries operational weight. A copilot helps a manager draft a performance review. An agent gathers the employee's goal progress, recent feedback, learning completions, and skills data, synthesizes it into talking points, and suggests follow-up actions like updating a development goal or scheduling a coaching conversation. The manager's role shifts from data assembly to decision-making.

Joule's architecture: why embedded matters

Most enterprise AI copilots sit on top of business applications. They access data through APIs, generate responses in a separate interface, and operate at arm's length from the processes they are meant to improve. This architecture creates a fundamental ceiling: the AI can inform decisions but cannot participate in them.

Joule's architecture is different in a way that matters operationally. It is embedded directly into SAP Business Suite, not bolted on, but woven into the transaction layer of SuccessFactors, S/4HANA, Ariba, Concur, and SAP Analytics Cloud.[5] This means Joule operates within the same security model, the same role-based permissions, and the same workflow logic that governs every human user.

This design choice addresses a critical challenge: nearly half of organizations cite data searchability (48%) and data reusability (47%) as obstacles to their AI automation strategy.[4] By operating within SAP's data layer rather than above it, Joule avoids the integration bottleneck that hampers many enterprise AI deployments.

SAP states that Joule now supports 80% of the most-used tasks across SAP systems, is available in 11 languages, and integrates with Microsoft 365 Copilot, allowing users to access SAP intelligence directly from Outlook and Teams without navigating to SuccessFactors.[6] The Microsoft integration signals that agents are no longer confined to the system they were built in.

The integration with SAP's existing access controls is worth emphasizing. An AI that recommends a successor from outside your permissions context is a liability. An AI that respects the same role-based permission structure as your HRBP, seeing only what that role should see, acting only within those boundaries, is something an organization can actually govern.

The agent roster: what Joule does in HR today

It is easy to dismiss agents as vaporware if you only read roadmap slides. Here is what has shipped and what has firm delivery dates.

Performance and Goals Agent (generally available, November 2025). This was the first Joule Agent purpose-built for HR.[7] Rather than waiting for a manager to ask for performance data, the agent proactively gathers goals, achievements, feedback, and review history for each direct report. It synthesizes this into personalized discussion points and suggests follow-up actions: schedule a one-on-one, update a development goal, adjust a rating based on new evidence. The agent transforms performance conversations from administrative exercises into strategic coaching moments.

Four additional agents (expected general availability, May 2026):[8]

The Career and Talent Development Agent automates succession planning and helps managers identify future leaders. It reasons over existing succession plans, prompts managers for updates when plans go stale, recommends successors based on skills and experience data, and generates personalized development plans. The operative word is "reasons", this is not a report. It is an agent that evaluates the quality of your succession pipeline and acts when it finds gaps.

The HR Service Agent functions as a first line of response for employee questions about company policies, benefits, and processes. Before routing a question to a human, it searches company policy documents and knowledge bases. If it cannot resolve the query, If it cannot resolve the query, it raises a ticket in SAP SuccessFactors Enterprise Service Management.

The People Intelligence Agent connects workforce analytics in SAP Business Data Cloud with Joule's conversational interface. HR leaders can ask questions in natural language, about attrition risk, skills distribution, or capability gaps, and receive visual analytics with recommendations, not just data tables.

The Payroll Agent addresses one of the most common sources of employee frustration: understanding pay. Building on SAP's existing Explain Pay feature in Joule, which integrates data from SuccessFactors time tracking and reportedly reduces pay-related resolution time by up to 50%, the Payroll Agent extends this capability to both employees and payroll administrators, providing natural-language explanations for pay changes connected to specific time entries like overtime, absences, or shift differentials.[9]

At SAP Connect 2025, SAP announced more than a dozen new Joule Agents spanning finance, procurement, supply chain, and HR, part of a broader expansion that brought the total to more than 40 agents across SAP's portfolio.[10] The pattern across all of them is consistent: each agent is domain-specific, operates within enterprise security boundaries, and is designed to reason and act, not just respond.

Additionally, SAP has introduced a workforce knowledge network that integrates third-party content from industry experts, including G-P (Globalization Partners) and The Josh Bersin Company, directly into Joule, enabling agent-to-agent interaction that grounds recommendations in external research and best practices.[11]

Joule Studio: building custom agents

Ready-made agents address common workflows. But every organization has processes that are unique, and this is where Joule Studio enters the picture.

Joule Studio, a capability within SAP Build, reached general availability for skill building in July 2025 and for agent building in January 2026.[12] It allows organizations to create custom Joule agents and skills using a low-code interface, grounded in SAP's Knowledge Graph and business data.

Two capabilities make Joule Studio strategically significant.

First, Model Context Protocol (MCP) support allows custom agents to connect with external tools and non-SAP systems through an open standard.[13] This is not a proprietary integration framework. An HR agent built in Joule Studio can pull compensation benchmarking data from a third-party system and combine it with internal SuccessFactors data to make a recommendation, breaking down the data silos that consistently impede AI value.

Second, multi-agent orchestration allows agents to function as tools for one another. Joule can invoke a recruiting agent and a workforce planning agent in sequence to solve a problem that spans both domains, identifying a skills gap, finding internal candidates, and flagging external hiring needs in a single workflow.[14] This coordination is what distinguishes an agent ecosystem from a collection of disconnected tools.

Early adopters are building production-grade agents quickly. At SAP's Hack2Build event in January 2026, partner teams, including Capgemini, which described Joule Studio as enabling connectivity across SAP and non-SAP environments in hybrid landscapes, created functional custom agents in under seven days.[15]

The governance gap

The capability is moving fast. The governance is not.

The data on this point is unambiguous. While 74% of companies expect to use agentic AI within two years, only 21% have a mature model for governance of autonomous agents.[1] The risks that concern organizations most all relate to governance: data privacy and security (73%), legal and regulatory compliance (50%), and governance capabilities and oversight (46%).[1]

For HR, the governance challenge is acute. AI agents in HR make decisions that affect people's careers, compensation, development opportunities, and access to roles. An agent that recommends successors shapes who gets developed. An agent that answers policy questions defines how policy is interpreted. An agent that explains pay determines whether employees trust their employer.

These are not edge cases. They are the core of what HR does.

A minimum viable governance model for agentic AI in HR should address several critical dimensions:

Permissions inheritance. Role-based permissions must extend cleanly to agents, if a manager cannot see certain employee data, their agent cannot either. SAP has built this into Joule's architecture through principal propagation, meaning the user's identity is passed end-to-end so that any agent action runs within that user's security context.[5]

Human-in-the-loop checkpoints. High-stakes decisions, succession nominations, compensation recommendations, talent mobility, require defined approval points. Joule Studio enables human-in-the-loop by default.[12]

Auditability. Organizations must be able to explain why an agent made a specific recommendation. This is particularly important as regulatory frameworks like the EU AI Act impose transparency requirements on high-risk AI systems.

Bias monitoring. When agents influence talent decisions at scale, bias monitoring must be operationalized, not just discussed. This is especially critical for agents involved in succession planning, career development, and internal mobility recommendations.

Platform guardrails are necessary but not sufficient. The harder work is organizational: defining which decisions agents can make autonomously, which require human approval, and how to measure whether agents are making those decisions fairly.

The economic case

The investment case for agentic AI in HR is strengthening, but executives should approach the numbers with appropriate nuance.

SAP's Value of AI Report, conducted with Oxford Economics in 2025, found that organizations are seeing an average 16% ROI on AI investments (approximately $4.7 million), with expectations for that to nearly double to 31% within two years. Seventy-nine percent expect positive ROI in less than three years.[16]

However, Deloitte's research provides important counterweight. Most organizations achieve satisfactory ROI on AI use cases within two to four years, significantly longer than the seven-to-twelve–month payback period typically expected for technology investments.[17] And 65% of respondents in SAP's own study acknowledge they are unsure or do not believe AI is delivering its full potential, even as they report positive returns.[16]

The reconciliation is straightforward. AI delivers measurable productivity gains on narrow use cases, drafting, summarization, Q&A automation, relatively quickly. The transformative impact, redesigning processes, redeploying talent, making better strategic decisions, takes longer and requires organizational change that most companies are still building.

For HR leaders, the practical implication is a dual-speed strategy. Capture quick wins with copilot-level capabilities (Joule answering employee queries, drafting performance reviews, explaining pay) while investing in the data quality, governance, and change management required for agent-level capabilities (automated succession planning, skills-based talent recommendations, workforce analytics on demand).

What this means for the HR operating model

The deeper question is not whether to deploy Joule agents, but what happens to HR when agents handle an increasing share of operational work.

Deloitte and ServiceNow's 2026 enterprise research describes a future where employees increasingly serve as "orchestrators and supervisors of AI agents rather than direct executors of tasks."[18] For HR, this implies a structural shift in how the function allocates its capacity.

Consider the HR Service Center. Today, a significant portion of HR service delivery involves answering repetitive questions, leave policies, benefits eligibility, payroll explanations, onboarding logistics. If an HR Service Agent handles a majority of these queries autonomously, the freed capacity creates space for HR professionals to focus on strategic workforce planning, manager coaching, organizational design, and the contextual employee support that no agent can replicate.

SAP itself is modeling this internally. Its People & Culture Lounge concept, announced in late 2025, provides all 110,000 SAP employees access to one-on-one conversations with HR experts. The rationale is explicit: because technologies like Joule resolve standard inquiries efficiently, personal conversation in complex situations becomes more important, not less.[19]

The pattern is instructive: agents handle volume. Humans handle judgment. The organizations that get the division of labor right will have HR functions that are both more efficient and more human, not one at the expense of the other.

A practical path forward

For SuccessFactors customers evaluating Joule's agentic capabilities, the path forward requires sequencing technology, data, and governance in the right order.

Start with data readiness, not agent deployment. Every agent is only as good as the data it reasons over. If your job architecture is inconsistent, your skills taxonomy is unmaintained, or your performance data is sparse, agents will produce unreliable recommendations, and users will lose trust before the technology gets a chance to prove itself. Clean your Talent Intelligence Hub data. Ensure Growth Portfolios are populated. Validate that role-based permissions are correctly configured.

Activate Joule and capture copilot-level value now. Joule's conversational capabilities, answering employee questions, drafting content, navigating SuccessFactors, are available today and deliver immediate productivity returns. Build user familiarity and organizational comfort with AI in HR workflows before introducing agents that act autonomously.

Deploy the Performance and Goals Agent as your first agent. It is generally available, scoped to a well-understood process, and delivers value that managers will notice immediately. Use it to establish governance patterns, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, feedback mechanisms, monitoring protocols, that you can extend to more complex agents later.

Build governance before you need it. Define which talent decisions require human approval. Establish auditability standards for agent recommendations. Create bias monitoring protocols for agents that influence succession, development, and career pathing. The May 2026 wave of agents will arrive whether you are ready or not.

Plan for Joule Studio when ready-made agents are not enough. If your organization has unique workflows, custom onboarding sequences, industry-specific compliance processes, bespoke talent review cycles, Joule Studio allows you to create custom agents grounded in your business data. But this is a Phase 2 capability. Get the fundamentals right first.

Where to start

The trajectory is unmistakable. Joule is evolving from a conversational interface into an agentic layer that will increasingly participate in, not just support, HR decision-making. The Performance and Goals Agent is the first tangible proof point. The Career and Talent Development, HR Service, People Intelligence, and Payroll agents arriving in 2026 will extend this pattern across the HR lifecycle.

But the technology is ahead of most organizations' readiness. The data is clear: agentic AI governance lags adoption by a wide margin. The organizations that invest now in data quality, permissions architecture, and governance frameworks will be positioned to capture value from agents as they mature. Those that wait for agents to force the issue will spend their time managing risk instead of creating value.

Joule is not a chatbot. Increasingly, it is not even a copilot. It is becoming a new category of HR participant, one that reasons, acts, and learns within the same systems your people use every day. The question for HR leaders is not whether to engage with this shift. It is whether they will shape it, or be shaped by it.

What is your organization's readiness for agentic AI in HR? I'd welcome a conversation, particularly from those who have deployed the Performance and Goals Agent or are planning for the May 2026 wave.

References

 

  1. Deloitte, State of AI in the Enterprise, 2026 Edition
  2. SAP, SAP Announces New Generative AI Assistant Joule
  3. Deloitte, Tech Trends 2026
  4. Deloitte, Agentic AI Strategy, Tech Trends 2026
  5. SAP, Joule Artificial Intelligence Assistant
  6. SAP, What's New with SAP Business AI, Joule, and Joule Agents in the SAP SuccessFactors 2H 2025 Release
  7. SAP, Innovation Guide H2 2025
  8. SAP, New Joule Agents and Embedded Intelligence Supercharge Business Returns
  9. SAP, Agent Builder in Joule Studio Is Now Generally Available
  10. SAP, Build Business-Grounded AI Agents, Faster
  11. SAP, Unprecedented Possibilities: New SAP SuccessFactors Innovations
  12. SAP, Joule Studio
  13. SAP, Build Business-Grounded AI Agents, Faster
  14. SAP, Partner-Driven Innovation Takes Off with Agent Builder in Joule Studio
  15. SAP and Oxford Economics, The Value of AI Report
  16. Deloitte, AI ROI: The Paradox of Rising Investment and Elusive Returns
  17. Deloitte and ServiceNow, The AI-Fueled Enterprise of 2026
  18. SAP, More Than Just Technology: Why Personal HR Support Gains Importance in the Age of AI

entry.title: Week 4 : Use Joule Predictive Code Completion to Implement Determination
entry.content:
Congratulations to everyone who came along with us till the 4th week of the ABAP Developer Challenge. So far we have seen :

  • How to create a OData UI Service from Scratch using Joule Chat
  • How to use Joule - Explain feature to understand a block of code.
  • How to use Joule - RAP Predict Business Logic to create logic for specific tasks.

Let us wrap up with another interesting feature of Joule's ABAP AI Capability - The Joule Predictive Code Completion.

Quick Links

Please use this separate thread to ask your questions and discuss issues. 

Sample Scenario

We had asked you to create a field of type 'Date' during the RAP application creation.

Now let us use Determination function to calculate the number of years since the value in Date field. You can implement it while creating a new record using the app.

Task1: Create a determination and implement the corresponding method.

  1. Define a determination in the BDEF of the CDS view.Define Determination
  2. Now you can notice a small icon to the left of the determination definition. Click on it to create the implementation method for adding determination logic in the local handler class of the behavior pool of your entity. Save and activate the changes.Implement DeterminationFor additional information on Determinations and its syntax and use you can refer the BDEF ‘/DMO/R_Travel_D’. Alternatively you can also refer https://github.com/SAP-samples/abap-platform-rap100/blob/main/exercises/ex04

Task2: Enhance determination with Joule Code Prediction Capability

Use Joule's Predictive Code Completion feature to complete your code by providing simple prompts for each step in the logic.

Here please note that we should use the Toggle option to disable / enable Joule Code Prediction Capability by pressing  button in the toolbar.

Hint: Disable the Joule Code Prediction Capability if you want to give prompts for new steps / once finished to stop Automatic Code Prediction.

Note:- The logic and examples are mentioned just for your reference and to make the purpose of the challenge easy to understand. Please feel free to use your own logic and dataset.

To know how to use Predictive Code Completion follow this tutorial

Please note that the challenge will remain open for another week for anyone who is running late. 

Before we close for this year we would like to thank everyone who participated. We hope all of you enjoyed the challenge. Let us continue to explore how SAP Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities can support us with our day-to-day tasks when working with ABAP code, to increase our efficiency and to lower total cost of development.

Happy Jouling!!!

entry.title: Learn what makes your business work anywhere with SAP’s mobile apps, AI, and SAP BTP
entry.content:
SAP mobile apps with Joule integrationAbout 230 million cloud users worldwide use SAP solutions, running on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP). SAP's data and business process expertise is unparalleled. However, the possibilities to benefit from this business power everywhere using mobile consumer devices are far from being exhausted by all customers.

A new course on learning.sap.com shows in an easily comprehensible way how to get started with mobile apps from SAP for smart phones, tablets, watches, or mixed-reality headsets.

Discovering SAP's Mobile Experiences is the first version of a learning journey that illustrates why the SAP Mobile Start app and SAP's AI Copilot Joule are excellent entry points for SAP users in many business environments for using powerful features on simple-to-use mobile devices.

The approximately one-hour-long training offers an overview of all available mobile apps provided by SAP for its solutions, and how managing as well as developing further native apps based on SAP BTP works.

Challenges for businesses, opportunities for mobile applications

With a well-orchestrated mobile setup, getting daily tasks done becomes faster and easier for roles such as supply chain managers, mechanics, engineers, finance managers, and many more. Especially in challenging environments, SAP mobile apps accompany employees with deep AI integration and supporting features for simplified and hands-free operation. With additional development possibilities on SAP BTP, customers or partners can extend what’s already been offered by SAP and even build their own app suites, massively simplified and accelerated by the platform's AI coding capabilities.

All of this, shows the training, makes SAP’s mobile offerings a vital part of any digital transformation strategy.

The first version of Discovering SAP's Mobile Experiences provides separate lessons for the powerful SAP Mobile Start and SAP Service and Asset Manager apps. Further chapters on mobile native apps for business travelers, HR, finance will follow soon.

Discovering SAP's Mobile Experiences is publicly available now and soon also accessible via sap.com/mobile.

entry.title: The AI Revolution in CX: How SAP Business AI is Redefining the Customer Journey
entry.content:
Today’s customers demand intelligent, personalized experiences. To deliver, businesses need more than just good intentions—they need powerful, embedded intelligence. SAP is delivering this with SAP Business AI, a comprehensive strategy that infuses the entire Customer Experience (CX) suite with practical, powerful artificial intelligence, fronted by its conversational copilot, Joule.

This isn't just about AI for your customers; it's about AI for your entire organization.

 AI for Your Frontline Teams: Smarter Engagement

SAP Business AI works out-of-the-box to make your daily operations more intelligent. With Joule as their copilot, your teams can work faster and smarter.

  • For Sales: Joule drafts personalized emails and summarizes opportunities, while embedded AI scores leads so reps can focus on the deals most likely to close.
  • For Service: Joule instantly summarizes complex customer tickets, and AI recommends the best knowledge base solutions, slashing resolution times and boosting satisfaction.
  • For Commerce: AI-powered search and product recommendations create a bespoke shopping experience for every single visitor, driving conversion and loyalty.

AI for Your Project Teams: Faster Implementations

SAP’s AI vision goes deeper, empowering the people who build your CX solutions with Joule for Consultants (J4C).

Think of J4C as an expert "AI librarian" for your project team. It’s a specialized copilot that provides instant, conversational access to SAP's vast knowledge base. Consultants can ask for best practices, configuration guidance, and even code intelligence. By mastering smart prompting, teams can dramatically reduce project complexity and accelerate delivery with confidence.

Ready to Master SAP Business AI?

Dive deep by enrolling in the official SAP Learning course and earning your Badge!

Introducing SAP Business AI for SAP Customer Experience

entry.title: Artificial Intelligence in Organizational Change Management: From Theory to Practice
entry.content:
As a quick recap: in our previous post, we explored the impact of AI across our six

Organizational Change Management (OCM) dimensions:  In our previous post (read the article here), we explored HOW AI impacts the six OCM dimensions.

Now let's get practical: WHAT can you do with it - now?

Based on conversations with change professionals across industries, we've identified three high-impact opportunities where AI is already delivering results in SAP transformations.

Being in mutual exchange with other change professionals, we have identified many opportunities for the improvement of transformation & change projects by using AI capabilities to solve several challenges.

From streamlining content creation to enabling impactful communication on tight budgets and acting as a strategic sparring partner, AI opens new possibilities for tackling challenges and driving innovation in OCM. Below, you’ll find a graphic that presents the three opportunities at a glance.

 

 

 

 

 

1st Opportunity – Transforming Content Creation with AI

Scenario
Your teams produce great ideas—but turning workshop outcomes into on‑brand, stakeholder‑ready assets is slow, manual, inconsistent, and scattered across channels. Deadlines stack up, style guides get bent, and version control becomes a guessing game. You need a way to turn raw inputs into professional, audience‑specific materials without the heavy lifting.

AI Solution
Use generative AI to turn workshop outputs, briefs, and project artifacts into on‑brand deliverables: vision statements, key messages, stakeholder briefings, enablement outlines, and even first‑cut engagement plans. Configure brand and tone guardrails, map stakeholder preferences, and let AI adapt content by audience, channel, and language —reducing formatting work while improving consistency.

Real Impact
You can move from raw inputs to polished, customer‑specific content faster, keep materials aligned to your brand and narrative, and tailor messages to stakeholder needs with less manual effort. Content creation becomes a coordinated flow across change strategy, communication, and execution—supporting momentum throughout the program.

Getting Started
Collect a small set of canonical inputs (brand guidelines, message house, workshop notes, key slides) and define master prompts and templates for each asset type. Pilot with two priority deliverables—such as a vision statement and a stakeholder briefing—keep a human review step for accuracy and tone and expand to additional channels once the workflow proves reliable.

 

2nd Opportunity – Realizing appealing communication with tight budgets 

Scenario
The plan is solid, the milestones are set—but the budget for agencies and production support is too expensive. You still need a steady cadence of videos, posts, articles, and emails that land with different audiences and regions. The choice shouldn’t be “go quiet” or “burn out the team.”

AI Solution
Use generative AI to transform SAP standard content and your project artifacts (charters, release notes, FAQs, town‑hall transcripts) into ready‑to‑use assets—short explainer videos, community posts, newsletters, and stakeholder emails. Apply brand and tone guardrails, translate or reuse for key markets, and generate channel‑specific variants with approvals routed to your team.

Real Impact
You can maintain a consistent communications drumbeat without additional headcount, reach more audiences in their preferred channels and languages, and keep messages aligned to project milestones—preserving quality while staying within budget.

Getting Started
Identify your source content and priority channels, define brand/voice prompts and approval rules, and pilot two to three asset types (e.g., a monthly newsletter, a short video, and a stakeholder email). Keep human review for accuracy and context, then expand once the workflow feels reliable.

 

3rd Opportunity – Engage AI as your OCM sparring partner

Scenario
You’ve got strong change ideas, but not always the time—or the bench—to test them, iterate fast, and pressure‑test assumptions. Workshops end, momentum fades, and decisions wait for the next availability slot. You need a trusted counterpart that can challenge thinking and turn concepts into actionable plans on demand.

AI Solution
Set up an AI assistant grounded in your internal playbooks, past project lessons, and SAP good practices. Use it to iterate on change narratives, draft stakeholder maps and engagement plans, compare scenario options, summarize feedback patterns, and propose next‑best actions—available on demand, 24/7.

Real Impact
You can move from idea to plan faster, explore more options before committing, and document the rationale behind decisions. The assistant augments your expertise with rapid synthesis and structured recommendations, while you retain judgment and sign‑off.

Getting Started
Curate a safe, high‑quality knowledge base (frameworks, project docs, templates, lessons learned), define clear prompts and review criteria, and begin with co‑drafting tasks such as sponsor briefings or comms outlines. Validate outputs with your team, then extend to scenario testing and decision support as confidence grows.

Looking ahead

AI brings new momentum to how we deliver change—but its impact depends on how intentionally we choose to work with it. Start small with a clearly scoped use case, define success metrics, and iterate based on evidence. Keep experts in the loop throughout: AI accelerates first drafts, clustering, and signal detection, while humans ensure narrative, nuance, and sound decision‑making. Establish privacy, security, and responsible‑AI guardrails from day one to build trust and transparency.

But most importantly: don’t wait for “perfect AI.”
Experiment, learn, adapt—and share your experiences. Your insights help the wider change community move forward faster.

✓ Start with ONE high-pain use case (e.g., stakeholder analysis automation)

✓ Define success metrics upfront (time saved, quality scores, adoption lift)

✓ Keep humans in the loop: AI drafts, experts review and decide

✓ Establish guardrails: privacy, security, bias checks—non-negotiable from day one

We should not be waiting for perfect AI—keep experimenting, learning, and iterating.

If you want to go deeper or need support setting up your first AI‑powered OCM workflow, reach out via maja.polke@gmail.com or hannah.bruelke@gmail.com or explore our SharePoint site for templates, guides, and examples.

Together, let’s shape how AI elevates OCM—today and for the future.

entry.title: How Business Information Extraction in SAP Sales Cloud V2 Accelerates Sales Response and Conversion
entry.content:
What you will get from this blog

In this blog, you’ll understand how Business Information Extraction (BIE) powered by generative AI in SAP Sales Cloud V2 can turn unstructured inputs—like emails or requests—into structured, actionable sales data. More importantly, you’ll see how this capability directly impacts real business outcomes: faster response times, improved quote quality, reduced manual effort, and a more scalable sales process. By the end, you’ll have a clear view of how to translate AI into measurable value and how to easily activate it for your sales operations—not as a concept, but as a practical, ready-to-use AI-CRM case scenario.

 

Process story and business flow

A quote request rarely arrives in a perfect format.

More often, it comes as an email with a mix of free text, item references, quantities, delivery expectations and so on. Sometimes there is also extra context hidden in the wording: this is for a shutdown window, this should follow an existing agreement, or this request is linked to a wider project.

That is usually where the work begins.

Before pricing, validation, or commercial alignment even starts, someone has to read the request, identify what matters, interpret the context, and turn all of that into something structured enough to create a sales quote. In more complex buying environments, this is not unusual - customers may use different interaction channels during their buying journey, which helps explain why inbound email quote requests often arrive fragmented across formats and touchpoints rather than as a single clean transaction.

A typical example could be this:

Please quote 24 units of valve kit VK-220 and 12 filter assemblies FA-90 for our Porto facility. We need delivery before the maintenance shutdown in week 39. Please align pricing with our existing agreement and see the attached sheet for the full list.

This is where Business Information Extraction (BIE) in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 becomes relevant.

SAP documents BIE as the capability used to configure extraction logic for AI-assisted creation of sales quotes from emails, allowing organizations to define the scenario that interprets the incoming request and supports quote creation. 

So, in simple terms, the flow could look like this:

 

What changes here is not that the seller disappears from the process. What changes is that the seller can start from a better first draft instead of spending unnecessary time decoding the request.

Business benefits

The value of Business Information Extraction (BIE) goes beyond data extraction itself.
Its real impact lies in how it streamlines and improves the entire quote process, from request intake to final quote creation.

Each benefit shown below reflects a direct improvement in the flow once extraction is embedded early in the process:

 

 

Hence, BIE doesn’t just extract data  - it stabilizes, accelerates, and adapts the entire quote process end-to-end.

Use case scenarios and the flexibility of elements and classes

This is where BIE becomes more interesting.

SAP’s configuration model is not just about extracting static fields. It is built around three design components:

  • Elements, which represent explicit values to be extracted - I.e for quotes: Product ID, Product Name, Quantity and unit, request date and custom elements tailored to specific business needs, such as customer reference numbers.
  • Classes, which represent values derived from the content - Delivery Priority and custom classes tailored to specific business needs, such as customer type, request category, product family, and so on.
  • As a result of combining both elements and classes - Flexible scenarios can be defined, bringing these components together into a structured, business-relevant use case that adapts the extraction logic to specific processes and requirements.
     

That means the email-to-quote use case can be adapted depending on how the business actually receives requests.

Therefore, a simple scenario may focus on the elements and classes:

  • Element: Product ID
  • Element: Product Name
  • Element: Quantity
  • Element; Requested Date
  • Classes: Delivery Priority (Urgent request, Right away)

and that alone can already reduce a lot of manual effort!

But many organizations will want to go further. For example, a more operational quote flow could include elements such as:

  • Customer material number
  • contract reference
  • asset or serial reference

And it could include classes such as:

  • request type: new product, replacement
  • delivery priority: urgent, standard, planned
  • commercial context: contract-linked, project-driven, ad hoc
  • product family: spare part, accessory, consumable, finished good

This is where the “configurable to each customer’s specific business scenario” message really matters. The same BIE capability can be adapted to a customer that mostly handles recurring replenishment requests, and also to one that works with more project-heavy, location-specific, or contract-sensitive quote motions. Hence, SAP Sales Cloud V2 administrators configure the scenario used for AI-assisted sales quotes from emails, which is what makes this flexibility operationally meaningful rather than just conceptual.

Value accelerator path for easy activation

Understanding the use case is important, but making it easy to activate is what turns the idea into something real. The value accelerator path below brings together the main prerequisites, configuration steps, and scenario design choices needed to enable Business Information Extraction for AI-assisted email-to-quote.

See further about the prerequisites and configuration steps HERE.

​Addressing Business KPIs and Measuring outcomes early

Activating the use case is only part of the story. The next step is showing that it is improving the quote process in a visible and measurable way.

A practical starting point is to focus on four simple KPIs:

  • Time from request receipt to quote draft
    to show whether the intake process is becoming faster
  • Manual effort per quote
    to understand whether sellers spend less time on repetitive data capture
  • Rework caused by intake errors
    to track whether avoidable corrections are going down
  • First-pass quote completeness and tailored to your business need
    to see whether the first version of the quote is stronger, more complete and aligned to your business requirement

Together, these indicators help connect the use case to real operational outcomes, not just technical activation.

The best approach is to keep the first measurement cycle simple: start with one bounded scenario, define a baseline, measure the early impact, and refine from there. That kind of phased activation is usually the fastest way to make the use case real without turning it into a long design exercise.

 

Final thoughts

Business Information Extraction for email-to-quote helps bridge a common gap in the quote process: the gap between how customers send requests and how sales teams need that information to exist inside CRM.

That is what makes the use case compelling. It is not just an AI feature layered on top of a sales process. It is a practical mechanism to reduce friction where friction already exists, at the point where unstructured demand first enters the business.

And that is also why the configurability matters so much. The real strength is not only that BIE can extract data from unstructured emails, but that it can be shaped around the customer’s own quote scenario through the right combination of elements, classes, and scenario design. SAP’s current documentation supports setting up the model directly for AI-assisted sales quote creation from emails.

For teams working with SAP Sales Cloud V2, this makes BIE a very credible use case to activate: practical, business-relevant, configurable, and measurable. 

Thanks for reading!

entry.title: SAP MDG News: AI Use Cases delivered, demoed and presented
entry.content:
Last week, I had the opportunity to present the latest SAP MDG and AI developments to a major client. Since the 2023 release, SAP MDG has unlocked numerous AI use cases, with most integrated directly into Joule and additional embedded AI features available. The live demo showcased all these capabilities in action.

Key AI Use Cases Overview

We're currently seeing three interaction types of Joule with MDG that are transforming how users work with master data:

Use Case 1: Informational

Knowledge at Your Fingertips: With Joule integrated into the SAP Launchpad, business users can ask MDG-related questions without leaving their current workspace. Instead of opening external browsers or applications, users can simply ask questions like "Does MDG support parallel processes for the same object?" Joule accesses the complete SAP Help documentation and SAP Community to deliver high-quality answers instantly.

Use Case 2: Navigational

Smart Data Discovery: Users can pose specific questions about master data within their MDG system. For example, asking "Show me all Business Partners with NWT in their name" displays results directly within the Joule chatbot. Users can then navigate directly from Joule to the appropriate app or action, eliminating the traditional process of opening the correct app, searching for business partners, and then proceeding with tasks.

 

Use Case 3: Transactional

Guided Process Creation: This powerful capability is already available in MDG cloud ready mode. When creating a new Business Partner via Joule, users interact within the chatbot interface, receiving guided assistance with the right questions for BP creation. The system also provides duplicate detection alerts directly within the chat experience

Custom AI Solutions Discussion

Beyond standard use cases, we explored customer-specific implementations using SAP Business AI to enhance master data quality. The concept of an "Attribute Recommendation Agent" could automatically improve data quality through regular evaluation and remediation processes, keeping master data current and accurate through automated quality checks.

Client Feedback Section

The feedback on delivered use cases was very positive! We also discussed exciting possibilities for implementing custom AI use cases with SAP Business AI to further enhance their master data governance processes.

Call to Action

For more detailed insights, check out this comprehensive blog: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/meet-joule-your-ai-copilot-for-sap-master-data-governance/ba-p/14273811

Meet Joule - Your AI Copilot for SAP Master Data Governance 🤖

Best regards,

Steffen
